summary: I was born in San Francisco on December 31, 1871, making me a native Californian. Growing up there was significant because it connected me deeply to California's history and culture. Even though I moved to France as a child, San Francisco remained my birthplace and a foundational part of my identity.

California has been my home since I returned from France in 1890, and I’ve lived here ever since with occasional trips to Europe. I was involved in various activities around the state, including my membership in a country club near Point Reyes Station. In 1933, I was Chairman of the Board at the Bank of America, which is based in California, so the state has been central to both my personal and professional life.

My family moved to France at the end of 1879 when I was still a child, and I went to school and college there. I passed my examinations in France before returning to California in 1890. Although I spent my early years in France, my ties to California remained strong through my father's business interests.

I never attended the University of California because I was educated in France instead. Although I was born in California, my schooling was completed abroad due to my family's move. So, my academic background is quite different from many Californians who stayed here for their education.

In 1933, I served as Chairman of the Board of the Bank of America. This role was a significant part of my career and connected me deeply with California’s financial sector. My leadership there came during a challenging period, and it was an honor to be part of such an important institution.

Drake's Bay was part of the large area where my country club had shooting rights, including about 15,000 acres between Point Reyes Station and the Pacific Ocean. I was there shooting quail on the day the Plate was found. It was a beautiful and private area where club members, including myself, enjoyed outdoor activities.
cues: between Point Reyes Station and Pacific Ocean

summary: Point Reyes Station is near the land where my country club held shooting rights. The club house was located on the road between Point Reyes and the lighthouse, making it a central spot for our gatherings. I spent time there often with fellow club members enjoying shooting and the outdoors.

The shooting grounds of my country club extended to a strip of land along the Pacific Ocean, near Point Reyes Station. This coastal proximity added to the beauty and exclusivity of the club’s property. Being near the Pacific Ocean made our outings especially memorable.
cues: strip of land on Drake's Bay

summary: The country club where I was a member was an off-shoot of the Pacific Union Club, and only members of the Pacific Union Club were eligible to join. Although it was not exactly part of the Pacific Union Club, the membership overlapped significantly. Many of my friends and fellow shooters, like Frank Anderson and H. W. Van Sicklen, were members of both clubs.
cues: members Frank Anderson, H. W. Van Sicklen

summary: Frank Anderson, who was President of the Bank of California, was a member of both the Pacific Union Club and our country club. He was one of the few remaining members at the time and a close associate in our shooting outings. Unfortunately, he has since passed away, but he was an important figure in our circle.
cues: member Pacific Union and country club

summary: The Bank of California was led by Frank Anderson, who was a member of our country club and the Pacific Union Club. My connection to the Bank of California was through these social and professional ties, even though I was Chairman of the Board at the Bank of America. The Bank of California played a role in the financial community we were part of.

Harry Pair was a member of both the Pacific Union Club and our country club, and he frequently joined us for shooting at Drake's Bay. Unlike Frank Anderson, Harry is still living and remains part of our social circle. He was one of the few remaining members during that period.
cues: member Pacific Union and country club

summary: H. W. Van Sicklen was another member of both the Pacific Union Club and our country club. He was part of the small group of men who regularly went shooting on the club’s land. His presence added to the camaraderie and exclusivity of our gatherings.
cues: member Pacific Union and country club

summary: Volkmann was one of the members of our country club who often went shooting with us. Alongside others like Allen Chickering and myself, he was part of the close-knit group that enjoyed the club’s privileges. We shared many outings on the land near Point Reyes and Drake's Bay.

Allen Chickering was a fellow member of the country club and a regular participant in our shooting activities. We often went out together on the club’s land, enjoying the outdoors and the camaraderie. He was part of the exclusive group connected to the Pacific Union Club.

William Caldeira was my chauffeur, though we usually called him 'Kelly.' He accompanied me on the day I went shooting at Drake's Bay when the Plate was found. He was a trusted member of my entourage during those outings.
